title: American meatloaf
subtitle: Baked beef and pork loaf with a sticky ketchup glaze
excerpt: The American diner and home-kitchen standard: a mixture of beef mince, pork mince, onion, egg, and breadcrumbs shaped into a loaf and baked with a ketchup, brown sugar, and mustard glaze that sets to a sticky, slightly sweet crust. Served in slices with mashed potato. Leftovers make excellent sandwiches the next day.
sourceNotes: Baked minced-meat loaves appear in American domestic cookery from the nineteenth century, with early recipes in Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544). The ketchup-glazed version became the mid-twentieth century American standard, shaped by the proliferation of ketchup as a household condiment and by the influence of mid-century American home economics teaching.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked meatloaf in slices for up to 3 months. Reheat from frozen in a 160°C oven, covered with foil, for 25 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The meatloaf can be shaped and refrigerated unbaked for up to 24 hours. Add the glaze and bake from cold, adding 10 minutes to the cooking time.

content.text: The combination of beef and pork is better than beef alone: the pork fat keeps the loaf moist as it bakes through. Breadcrumbs soaked in milk (a panade) also prevent the loaf from becoming dense and dry, binding the meat while keeping it tender. Do not overmix the meat mixture; treat it like burger mince and work it only until everything is combined.

The glaze goes on in two stages: once at the start and once at the halfway point, when the first coat has dried and set. The combination of ketchup, brown sugar, Worcestershire sauce, and mustard gives a sticky, sweet-sharp crust that is the defining feature of the dish. Rest the loaf for 10 minutes before slicing — cutting immediately causes the juices to run and the loaf to fall apart.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Soak the {{breadcrumbs-dried}} of breadcrumbs in the {{whole-milk}} of milk for 2 minutes. In a large bowl, combine the {{beef-mince}} of beef mince, {{pork-mince}} of pork mince, {{onion}}, {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, {{eggs}} eggs, soaked breadcrumbs, {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, salt, and pepper. Mix with your hands until just combined. Do not overwork. Transfer to a lined baking tray and shape into a loaf roughly 25 cm by 12 cm.

Mix together the {{ketchup}} of ketchup, {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar, {{wholegrain-mustard}} of wholegrain mustard, and 1 tsp {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce for the glaze. Spread half the glaze over the top and sides of the loaf. Bake for 30 minutes, then spread the remaining glaze over and return to the oven for a further 25 to 30 minutes, until the internal temperature reaches 70°C or the juices run clear when pierced in the centre. Rest for 10 minutes before slicing.

items.cause: Too lean a mince, or overcooked
items.fix: Use mince with at least 20% fat; check the temperature and remove at 70°C to avoid drying out

items.cause: Not enough binding (eggs, breadcrumbs) or sliced too soon
items.fix: Ensure the correct proportions of egg and breadcrumb, and rest for the full 10 minutes before cutting

items.symptom: Glaze burns before the loaf is cooked through
items.cause: Oven too hot, or first glaze applied too early
items.fix: Apply the first glaze after the first 10 minutes of cooking rather than at the start; cover loosely with foil if the top is browning too fast

items.symptom: Centre is still pink after the stated time
items.cause: Loaf shaped too thick, or oven running cool
items.fix: A thermometer is reliable here — 70°C internal confirms safe cooking; shape the loaf no more than 7 cm high

content.text: Meatloaf is one of the defining dishes of mid-twentieth-century American home cooking, shaped by Depression-era frugality (extending expensive meat with breadcrumbs), post-war domestic science teaching, and the rise of ketchup as a near-universal American condiment. It appears in virtually every American home-cooking collection from the 1940s onward and is one of those dishes that carries strong associations with specific households rather than restaurants — almost everyone who grew up in America eating home-cooked meals has a version of this that was their family's. The cold leftover slice on bread the next day is considered by many to be the point.
